refashioned: Every craft and every systematic inquiry, and likewise every action and deliberate pursuit, is thought to aim at some good. This is why the good has rightly been described as that at which all things aim. However, there is a notable difference among ends: some ends are the activities themselves, while others are products that exist apart from the activities that produce them. Where the ends are products distinct from the activities, these products are by nature better than the activities that produce them. Now, since there are many actions, crafts, and sciences, their ends are correspondingly many: the end of medicine is health, of shipbuilding a vessel, of military strategy victory, of household management wealth. But wherever such crafts fall under a single overarching discipline -- as bridle-making and the other crafts concerned with equipping horses fall under horsemanship, and horsemanship together with every military endeavor falls under strategy, and in the same way other crafts fall under yet other master disciplines -- in all such cases the ends of the master disciplines are to be preferred over the ends of the subordinate ones, since the subordinate ends are pursued for the sake of the master ends. It makes no difference whether the activities themselves are the ends of the actions, or whether some further product beyond the activities is the end, as in the crafts just mentioned.
elegant: Every craft, inquiry, action, and pursuit aims at some good -- which is why the good has been defined as that at which all things aim. Ends differ, however: some are the activities themselves, others are products beyond them, and products are superior to the activities that produce them. Since crafts are organized hierarchically, with subordinate disciplines serving master ones, the ends of master disciplines are always to be preferred, for it is for their sake that the subordinate ends are pursued.
summary: All human activities aim at some good, but goods are organized hierarchically. Subordinate crafts serve higher ones, and the ends of the higher crafts are always more important than those beneath them.
distilled: Every purposeful activity aims at some good, and goods are arranged in hierarchies where lesser ends serve greater ones.
notes: This is the famous opening line of the Nicomachean Ethics, establishing teleology (purpose-directedness) as fundamental to understanding human action. The Greek word 'telos' (end/purpose) is central throughout. -- The hierarchical ordering of crafts anticipates the argument in 1.2 that politics is the 'master art.' -- The distinction between activities that are their own ends (energeiai) and those producing separate products (poieseis) recurs in Book VI and in the Metaphysics (Theta 6). -- This passage parallels the opening of the Eudemian Ethics (I.1).

refashioned:
If, then, there is some end of everything we do that we desire for its own sake -- with everything else being desired for the sake of this -- and if we do not choose everything for the sake of something further (for then the chain would go on to infinity, making all desire empty and pointless), then clearly this ultimate end must be the good, indeed the chief good. Would not knowledge of this chief good have a profound influence on how we live? Would we not, like archers who have a target to aim at, be more likely to hit upon what is right? If so, we must try, at least in outline, to determine what this chief good is, and which science or discipline has it as its object.

It would seem to belong to the most authoritative and most truly master discipline. And political science appears to be of this nature; for it is political science that determines which disciplines should be studied in a city, which ones each class of citizens should learn, and to what extent. We see that even the most highly valued disciplines -- such as military strategy, household management, and rhetoric -- fall under its authority. Since political science makes use of all the other sciences and since it legislates about what we should do and what we should refrain from, its end must encompass the ends of all the others, so that this end must be the good for humankind. For even if the good is the same for a single individual and for a city-state, that of the city-state is clearly something greater and more complete, whether to achieve or to preserve. While it is worthwhile to achieve the good even for one person alone, it is nobler and more divine to achieve it for a nation or for city-states. These, then, are the aims of our inquiry, since it belongs to political science in one sense of that term.
elegant: If there is an ultimate end we desire for its own sake -- everything else being desired for the sake of it -- then this must be the chief good, and knowing it would guide our lives as a target guides an archer. This chief good belongs to the most authoritative discipline, which is political science, since it governs all other sciences and legislates what citizens should do. Its end encompasses every other end, making it the good for humankind. And while achieving this good for one person is worthwhile, achieving it for a city-state is nobler and more divine.
summary: There must be an ultimate good that all other goods serve, or else desire would regress infinitely and become pointless. This chief good falls under political science, the master discipline that governs all others and legislates for the city-state. Achieving the good for a whole community is nobler than achieving it for an individual.
distilled: The chief good -- the ultimate aim of all human action -- belongs to political science, which governs all other disciplines and pursues the good of the whole community.
notes: The 'archer' analogy is one of Aristotle's most famous images and recurs in ethical literature for centuries. -- The identification of ethics with political science reflects the Greek understanding that individual flourishing is inseparable from civic life; see also Politics I.2 (1253a), where man is called a 'political animal.' -- The infinite regress argument is a standard Aristotelian move; compare Metaphysics Alpha 2 (994a). -- The claim that the communal good is 'more divine' (theioteron) connects to Aristotle's teleological theology.

refashioned: Our discussion will be adequate if it achieves as much clarity as the subject matter allows, for the same degree of precision should not be expected in every inquiry, any more than in every product of craftsmanship. Now the subjects that political science investigates -- noble and just actions -- involve so much variety and inconsistency that they may seem to exist merely by convention rather than by nature. Even goods themselves produce a similar uncertainty, because they sometimes bring harm: people have been ruined by their wealth, and others by their courage. We must therefore be content, when speaking about such subjects and reasoning from such starting points, to indicate the truth roughly and in outline. When we speak about things that hold only for the most part, using premises of the same kind, we should expect conclusions of the same character. In this same spirit each of our statements should be received, for it is the mark of an educated person to seek only as much precision in each subject as the nature of that subject permits. It is equally foolish to accept merely probable reasoning from a mathematician and to demand rigorous proofs from an orator.
elegant: Our discussion need only be as precise as ethics allows, for noble and just actions involve too much variation for mathematical exactness. Goods themselves are uncertain -- wealth and courage can bring ruin. We must therefore reason roughly, from premises that hold for the most part, and accept conclusions of the same kind. The mark of an educated person is knowing how much precision each subject permits: it is as foolish to accept mere probability from a mathematician as to demand strict proof from an orator.
summary: Ethics cannot achieve mathematical precision because its subject matter -- noble and just actions -- is inherently variable. An educated person recognizes that different fields require different standards of exactness and does not demand more rigor than the subject allows.
distilled: The mark of an educated mind is knowing how much precision each subject permits, and ethics by nature allows only rough generalization.
notes: This methodological passage is one of the most cited in Aristotle's corpus. The principle of 'precision appropriate to the subject' (to akribes) is fundamental to Aristotle's epistemology. -- The claim that moral truths hold 'for the most part' (hos epi to polu) rather than universally is central to Aristotle's practical philosophy and distinguishes it from his theoretical sciences. -- The convention-vs-nature debate (nomos vs. physis) was a major theme among the Sophists and in Plato's dialogues (Republic, Gorgias). -- This passage influenced Thomas Aquinas's treatment of moral reasoning in the Summa Theologiae (I-II, q.94, a.4).

refashioned:
Each person judges well about the things they know, and in those domains they are a good judge. Someone educated in a particular subject judges that subject well; someone who has received an all-round education judges well in general. This is why a young person is not a suitable student of political science: they lack experience in the actions of life, and the discussions of this subject begin from and concern such actions. Furthermore, since young people tend to be led by their passions, their study will be empty and unprofitable, because the aim of this inquiry is not mere knowledge but action. And it makes no difference whether someone is young in years or youthful in character; the deficiency does not depend on age but on living and pursuing each successive goal as passion dictates. For such people, like those who lack self-control, knowledge brings no benefit. But for those who form their desires and act in accordance with a rational principle, knowledge about these matters will be enormously valuable.

Let these remarks about the student, the kind of treatment to expect, and the purpose of this inquiry serve as our preface.
elegant: One judges well only what one knows. A young person is therefore an unsuitable student of political science, lacking the life experience from which ethical discussions begin. Moreover, since the young are driven by passion and this inquiry aims at action rather than mere knowledge, their study will be fruitless. The deficiency is not about age but about character: anyone who lives at the mercy of passion gains nothing from ethical knowledge. Only those who desire and act according to rational principle will benefit.
summary: Ethical study requires life experience and emotional maturity, not just intellectual ability. Since ethics aims at action rather than mere knowledge, those who are governed by passion -- whether young in years or in character -- will gain nothing from it.
distilled: Ethics requires not just intellectual capacity but life experience and emotional maturity, since its aim is right action, not mere knowledge.
notes: The distinction between knowledge (gnosis) and action (praxis) as the aim of inquiry is fundamental to Aristotle's division of sciences into theoretical, practical, and productive (Metaphysics VI.1). -- The 'incontinent' (akratikos) person -- who knows the good but fails to do it -- is treated at length in Book VII. -- The claim that character rather than age determines readiness for ethical study challenges mere seniority as a qualification for wisdom. -- This passage establishes that the Nicomachean Ethics is a practical work: its purpose is to make us good, not merely to understand goodness.

refashioned: Let us resume our inquiry and state, given that all knowledge and every pursuit aims at some good, what it is that political science aims at and what is the highest good achievable through action. On the verbal level there is very broad agreement: both ordinary people and people of refinement say it is happiness, and they identify living well and doing well with being happy. But when it comes to what happiness actually is, they disagree, and ordinary people do not give the same answer as the wise. Ordinary people think happiness is something plain and obvious -- like pleasure, wealth, or honor -- and they disagree among themselves. Often even the same person identifies it with different things: with health when sick, with wealth when poor. Aware of their own ignorance, they admire those who proclaim some grand ideal beyond their comprehension. Some thinkers, moreover, believed that apart from these many particular goods there exists another good that is self-subsistent and is the cause of the goodness in all of them. To examine every opinion that has been held would be rather pointless; it is enough to examine those that are most widely held or that seem to have some rational basis.
elegant: Everyone agrees verbally that the highest good is happiness -- living well and doing well. But they disagree about what happiness actually is. Ordinary people identify it with pleasure, wealth, or honor, and even the same person shifts between these depending on circumstance. Some philosophers have posited a self-subsistent Good that causes goodness in all particular goods. Rather than examining every opinion, it suffices to consider those most widely held or most rationally defensible.
summary: While everyone agrees that the highest good is happiness, people disagree profoundly about what happiness consists in. Ordinary people equate it with pleasure, wealth, or honor, while some philosophers posit an abstract Form of the Good. Aristotle proposes to examine only the most prevalent and reasonable views.
distilled: Everyone calls the highest good 'happiness,' but they disagree fundamentally about what happiness actually is.
notes: This begins Aristotle's dialectical method (endoxa): surveying common and expert opinions before working toward the truth. -- The 'self-subsistent good' refers to Plato's Form of the Good (Republic VI, 508e-509b), which Aristotle critiques at length in 1.6 (1.9-1.12). -- The identification of happiness (eudaimonia) with 'living well and doing well' (eu zen kai eu prattein) reveals that eudaimonia is not a feeling but an activity. -- This passage sets up the survey of candidate lives (pleasure, politics, contemplation) in 1.5 (1.7-1.8).

elegant: We must distinguish between reasoning from first principles and reasoning toward them -- as Plato rightly noted. Since we must begin with what is known to us rather than what is known in the absolute sense, a student of ethics must already have been brought up in good habits. The fact is our starting point; if it is sufficiently clear, there is no initial need for the reason behind it. Those who lack good upbringing and cannot acquire these starting points should heed Hesiod: best is he who understands on his own; good is he who listens to wise counsel; useless is he who does neither.
summary: Ethical inquiry must begin with what is already known to us through experience, not with abstract first principles. This is why good moral upbringing is essential: it provides the factual starting points from which ethical reasoning proceeds.
distilled: Good moral upbringing provides the experiential starting points without which ethical reasoning cannot even begin.
notes: Plato's question about direction of inquiry appears in Republic VII (the 'Allegory of the Cave' and the 'Divided Line'). -- The distinction between 'known to us' (gnorimon hemin) and 'known without qualification' (gnorimon haplos) is fundamental to Aristotle's epistemology; see also Physics I.1 and Posterior Analytics I.2. -- The Hesiod quotation is from Works and Days, lines 293-297. Hesiod (c. 700 BCE) was one of the earliest Greek poets, a foundational figure alongside Homer. -- The emphasis on habituation (ethismos) as prior to rational understanding anticipates Book II's account of how virtue is acquired.

refashioned:
Let us, however, resume our discussion from where we digressed. Judging from the lives people actually lead, most people -- including those of the most ordinary sort -- seem, not without some justification, to identify the good, or happiness, with pleasure. This is why they love the life of enjoyment. There are, broadly speaking, three prominent types of life: the life of enjoyment, the political life, and the contemplative life. The masses are plainly slavish in their tastes, preferring a life fit for cattle, but they gain some support for their view from the fact that many people in positions of power share the tastes of Sardanapallus.

A consideration of the prominent types of life shows that people of superior refinement and active temperament identify happiness with honor, for this is, roughly speaking, the goal of the political life. But honor seems too superficial to be what we are looking for, since it is thought to depend on those who bestow it rather than on the person who receives it, whereas the good, we sense, should be something that belongs to a person and is not easily taken away. Moreover, people seem to pursue honor in order to be reassured of their own goodness: they seek to be honored by people of practical wisdom, among those who know them, and on the basis of their virtue. Clearly, then, according to these people at least, virtue is superior to honor. And perhaps one might suppose that virtue, rather than honor, is the end of the political life. But even this seems somewhat incomplete, for it appears possible to possess virtue while being asleep, or while being inactive throughout one's entire life, and further, while suffering the greatest misfortunes. No one would call a person living this way happy, unless they were defending a thesis at any cost. But enough of this, for the subject has been sufficiently discussed.

Third comes the contemplative life, which we will consider later.
elegant: Most people identify happiness with pleasure and prefer the life of enjoyment -- a slavish preference shared even by the powerful. Those of superior refinement identify happiness with honor, the goal of the political life. But honor depends on others, not on oneself, and the good should be something one's own. People pursue honor as confirmation of their virtue, suggesting virtue is superior to honor. Yet even virtue seems incomplete: one can possess it while asleep, inactive, or suffering greatly, and no one would call such a person happy. The contemplative life remains to be considered.
summary: Aristotle surveys three candidate lives: the life of pleasure (rejected as slavish), the political life aimed at honor (rejected because honor depends on others and points toward virtue), and the contemplative life (deferred). Even virtue alone is insufficient for happiness, since one can possess virtue while inactive or suffering.
distilled: Neither pleasure, honor, nor the mere possession of virtue can be happiness, since each fails to account for what we truly mean by living well.
notes: Sardanapallus (Ashurbanipal) was a legendary Assyrian king proverbial among the Greeks for luxury and self-indulgence; the epitaph attributed to him was 'Eat, drink, and play, for everything else is not worth this.' -- The three lives (bios apolaustikos, bios politikos, bios theoretikos) form a framework that structures the entire Ethics, with the contemplative life treated in Book X.6-8. -- The observation that one can possess virtue while asleep introduces the crucial distinction between hexis (stable disposition/state) and energeia (activity), which becomes central to the function argument in 1.7 (1.17-1.18). -- The political life devoted to honor connects to the discussion of megalopsychia (greatness of soul) in Book IV.3.

refashioned: The life of money-making is one pursued under compulsion, and wealth is clearly not the good we are seeking, for it is merely useful -- valuable only for the sake of something else. One might sooner regard the previously mentioned objects -- pleasure, honor, virtue -- as ends, since they are valued for their own sake. But it is evident that not even these are the ultimate end, despite the many arguments that have been advanced in their support. Let us set this subject aside, then.
elegant: Wealth is plainly not the highest good, since it is merely useful -- valued only for the sake of something else. The money-making life is pursued under compulsion, not freely chosen. Even pleasure, honor, and virtue, though valued for themselves, are not the ultimate end.
summary: Wealth cannot be the highest good because it is always pursued as a means to something else, never as an end in itself. The money-making life is compulsory rather than freely chosen.
distilled: Wealth is always a means to something else and therefore cannot be the highest good.
notes: The dismissal of wealth as merely instrumental (chresimon) reflects Aristotle's broader teleological framework: the good must be complete and self-sufficient, not a mere tool. -- This brief passage completes the survey of lives begun in 1.7. -- The theme of wealth as instrumental returns in Politics I.8-10, where Aristotle distinguishes natural household management from unnatural money-making (chrematistike). -- The 'compulsion' (anagke) of money-making contrasts with the freedom (eleutheria) that characterizes the best human life.

refashioned: It would perhaps be better to consider the universal good and discuss thoroughly what is meant by it, although this inquiry is made difficult by the fact that the theory of Forms was introduced by people dear to us. Yet it would seem to be our duty -- indeed it is required of us -- for the sake of maintaining the truth, to criticize even what touches us closely, especially since we are philosophers, that is, lovers of wisdom. For while both truth and friends are dear, reverence demands that we honor truth above our friends.
elegant: We should examine the universal good, even though the theory of Forms was introduced by our friends. Yet as philosophers -- lovers of wisdom -- we must honor truth above friendship.
summary: Aristotle announces he must critique the Platonic theory of a universal Form of the Good, even though Plato was his teacher. He asserts that truth must take precedence over personal loyalty.
distilled: Truth must be honored above friendship, even when criticizing the ideas of those we love.
notes: The 'friends' are Plato and the Platonists; Aristotle studied at Plato's Academy for nearly twenty years (c. 367-347 BCE). -- The famous principle 'amicus Plato, sed magis amica veritas' (Plato is dear, but truth is dearer) derives from this passage, though the Latin formulation is medieval. -- This passage begins Aristotle's extended critique of Plato's Form of the Good (1.9-1.12), which is one of his most sustained engagements with Platonic metaphysics in the Ethics. -- The critique of the Forms also appears in Metaphysics I.9, VII.13-16, and XIII-XIV.

refashioned:
The thinkers who introduced the theory of Forms did not posit Forms for classes within which they recognized priority and posteriority (which is why they did not maintain the existence of a Form embracing all numbers). But the term 'good' is used in the category of substance, in that of quality, and in that of relation; and substance -- that which exists in its own right -- is by nature prior to the relative (for the relative is like an offshoot or accident of being). So there could not be a single common Form set over all these goods.

Furthermore, since 'good' has as many senses as 'being' -- for it is used in the category of substance (as of God and of reason), in quality (as of the virtues), in quantity (as of the moderate amount), in relation (as of the useful), in time (as of the right opportunity), and in place (as of the right location), and so on -- it clearly cannot be something universally present in all cases as a single thing, for then it would not be found in all the categories but in only one.

Furthermore, since there would be a single science of all the things falling under one Form, there would have to be one science of all goods. But in fact there are many sciences even of goods falling under a single category -- for example, the right opportunity in war is studied by military strategy, in disease by medicine; and the moderate amount in food is studied by medicine, in exercise by the science of gymnastics.

One might also ask what the Platonists mean by 'a thing itself,' since in 'man himself' and in a particular man the account of what it is to be a man is one and the same. Insofar as they are both man, they will in no respect differ; and if so, neither will 'the good itself' and particular goods differ insofar as they are good. Nor will the good be any more truly good for being eternal, since something that lasts a long time is no whiter than something that lasts only a day.

The Pythagoreans seem to give a more plausible account of the good by placing 'the one' in the column of goods; and it is them that Speusippus appears to have followed.
elegant: The Platonists did not posit Forms for series with priority and posteriority, yet 'good' is used across multiple categories -- substance, quality, relation, quantity, time, place -- so no single Form can unify them all. Moreover, if one Form covered all goods, there would be one science of them, yet in fact there are many. The 'thing itself' adds nothing: man-himself and a particular man share the same definition of man, so the good-itself and particular goods do not differ as goods. Nor does eternal duration make something more good, just as lasting longer does not make something whiter. The Pythagoreans offer a more plausible account by placing 'the one' in the column of goods.
summary: Aristotle raises multiple objections to Plato's Form of the Good: 'good' is used across different categories (substance, quality, relation, etc.) so no single Form can encompass it; there is no single science of all goods; the Form of the Good adds nothing to particular goods; and eternal existence does not make something better.
distilled: There can be no single universal Form of the Good because 'good' is used in irreducibly different senses across different categories of being.
notes: The Platonic doctrine of Forms (eidoi/ideai) is most fully presented in the Republic, Phaedo, and Symposium. -- The argument from categories draws on Aristotle's own Categories, where he identifies substance, quantity, quality, relation, place, time, etc. -- Speusippus (c. 407-339 BCE) was Plato's nephew and successor as head of the Academy. -- The Pythagoreans maintained a 'Table of Opposites' with columns of good and bad, where the One, Limit, and Odd were on the good side; see Metaphysics I.5 (986a). -- The argument that eternal duration does not add to goodness targets the Platonic assumption that Forms, being eternal, are superior to their temporal instances.

refashioned:
But let us discuss these matters elsewhere. An objection to what we have said, however, can be identified: the Platonists have not been speaking about all goods. Rather, goods that are pursued and valued for their own sake are called good by reference to a single Form, while those that tend to produce, preserve, or protect these goods are called good in a secondary sense, by reference to the first kind.

Clearly, then, goods must be spoken of in two ways: some are good in themselves, others are good because of these. Let us separate things good in themselves from things merely useful and consider whether the former are called good by reference to a single Form. What sorts of things would count as good in themselves? Those that are pursued even when isolated from any further benefit -- such as intelligence, sight, certain pleasures, and honors? These, even if we also pursue them for the sake of something else, would still be placed among things good in themselves. Or is nothing other than the Form of Good itself good in its own right? In that case the Form would be empty of content.

But if the things we named are also good in themselves, then the account of goodness will have to appear as something identical in all of them, just as the account of whiteness is identical in snow and in white lead. Yet the accounts of honor, wisdom, and pleasure, precisely with respect to their goodness, are distinct and diverse. The good, therefore, is not some single common element corresponding to one Form.
elegant: The Platonists might reply that only goods pursued for their own sake fall under a single Form, while instrumental goods are called good in a secondary sense. But even among intrinsic goods -- intelligence, sight, pleasure, honor -- the accounts of their goodness are distinct and diverse, unlike whiteness which is the same in snow and white lead. Therefore goodness is not a single common element answering to one Form.
summary: Even if we limit the discussion to things good in themselves rather than merely useful goods, their goodness turns out to be diverse. Intelligence, sight, pleasure, and honor are each good in different ways, so there is no single account of goodness that unifies them under one Form.
distilled: Even things that are good in themselves are good in irreducibly different ways, so no single Form of the Good can account for them all.
notes: This continues the critique of Plato's Form of the Good begun in 1.9. -- The analogy with whiteness in snow and white lead (ceruse) illustrates what would have to be the case if the Form theory were correct: goodness would need a single, uniform account like whiteness. -- The distinction between intrinsic goods (pursued for their own sake) and instrumental goods (pursued for the sake of something else) becomes central to Aristotle's own positive account of happiness in 1.7 (1.14-1.16). -- This argument influenced later debates about the univocity vs. equivocity of 'good,' particularly in medieval Scholastic philosophy.

refashioned:
But then what do we mean by 'good'? Surely goods are not like things that merely happen to share the same name by coincidence. Are goods one by being derived from one source, or by all contributing to one end, or rather by analogy? For just as sight is to the body, so reason is to the soul, and so on in other cases.

But perhaps these questions had better be set aside for now, since precise treatment of them would be more appropriate to another branch of philosophy. The same goes for the Form of the Good: even if there is some single good that is universally applicable to all goods, or that exists separately and independently, it clearly could not be achieved or attained by human beings, and what we are now seeking is something attainable.

Perhaps, however, someone might think it worthwhile to know this universal good as a kind of pattern, reasoning that by having it we would better know what goods are good for us, and knowing them, would attain them. This argument has some plausibility, but it seems to conflict with how the actual sciences proceed. For all the sciences, though they aim at some good and seek to supply what is lacking, set aside knowledge of the universal Good. Yet it is unlikely that all practitioners of the arts would be ignorant of, and not even seek, so powerful an aid.

It is hard to see, moreover, how a weaver or a carpenter would be helped in their own craft by knowing 'the good itself,' or how someone who has contemplated the Form would become a better doctor or general. For a doctor does not study health in the abstract but the health of human beings, or rather the health of a particular patient -- it is individuals that a doctor heals. But enough of these topics.
elegant: What do we mean by 'good'? Perhaps goods are unified by analogy -- as sight is in the body, so reason is in the soul. But even if a universal Good exists, it is unattainable by humans, and we seek something achievable. Knowing the Form of the Good as a pattern might seem useful, but the actual sciences all proceed without it. A doctor does not study health in the abstract but treats particular patients. Knowledge of 'the good itself' would not make a better weaver, carpenter, or general.
summary: Even if a universal Form of the Good existed, it would be unattainable by humans and practically useless. The actual sciences and crafts all function without reference to it. A doctor treats particular patients, not abstract health -- practical knowledge, not knowledge of universals, is what makes practitioners effective.
distilled: Even if a universal Good existed, it would be unattainable and practically useless, since every craft and science deals with particular goods, not abstract ones.
notes: This concludes Aristotle's critique of the Platonic Form of the Good (1.9-1.12). -- The suggestion that goods are related 'by analogy' (kat' analogian) rather than by a single Form anticipates the medieval doctrine of the 'analogy of being' (analogia entis), developed especially by Aquinas. -- The 'another branch of philosophy' likely refers to metaphysics or first philosophy. -- The practical argument -- that knowledge of the Form does not help the craftsman -- is characteristically Aristotelian: theory must serve practice. -- This passage influenced the development of particularism in modern virtue ethics, especially in the work of John McDowell and Martha Nussbaum.

refashioned: Let us return once more to the good we are seeking and ask what it might be. It appears to be different in different actions and crafts: it is one thing in medicine, another in strategy, another in each of the other crafts. What, then, is the good of each? Surely it is that for the sake of which everything else in that domain is done. In medicine this is health, in strategy victory, in architecture a house, and in every other sphere something else. In every action and pursuit, the end is the good, for it is for the sake of this end that people do everything else they do. Therefore, if there is a single end for all that we do, this will be the good achievable by action; and if there are more than one, these will be the goods achievable by action.
elegant: The good differs across different actions and crafts: health in medicine, victory in strategy, a house in architecture. In each case the good is that for the sake of which everything else is done. If there is one ultimate end of all action, this is the good achievable by action; if there are several, these are the goods.
summary: The good varies across different domains -- it is health for medicine, victory for strategy. In each case, the good is the end for whose sake everything else is done. If all actions converge on a single ultimate end, that is the good achievable by action.
distilled: The good in any domain is the end for whose sake everything else is done, and if there is one ultimate end of all action, that is the highest good.
notes: This passage marks the transition from Aristotle's critique of Plato's Form of the Good to his own positive account. -- The teleological framework (everything aims at an end) was established in 1.1 and is now applied to identify the supreme good. -- The argument will narrow in 1.14 to show that among ends, some are more final than others, with happiness as the most final. -- The method of starting from particular cases and generalizing is characteristic of Aristotle's empirical approach; contrast Plato's top-down deduction from the Form of the Good.

refashioned:
So the argument, by a different route, has arrived at the same point. But we must try to state this even more clearly. Since there are evidently more ends than one, and we choose some of them -- such as wealth, flutes, and instruments generally -- for the sake of something else, clearly not all ends are ultimate ends. But the chief good is evidently something ultimate. Therefore, if there is only one ultimate end, this will be what we are seeking; and if there are more than one, the most ultimate among them will be what we are seeking.

Now we call that which is worthy of pursuit for its own sake more ultimate than that which is pursued for the sake of something else. And we call that which is never chosen for the sake of anything else more ultimate than things that are chosen both for themselves and for the sake of that other thing. Therefore, what we call ultimate without qualification is that which is always chosen for its own sake and never for the sake of something else.
elegant: Not all ends are ultimate -- we choose some, like wealth and tools, only for the sake of further ends. The chief good must be something truly ultimate. What is pursued for its own sake is more ultimate than what is pursued for the sake of something else, and what is never chosen as a means to anything further is ultimate without qualification.
summary: Since some ends are pursued only as means to further ends, not all ends are equally final. The chief good must be the most final end -- the one that is always chosen for its own sake and never as a means to anything else.
distilled: The chief good is that which is always chosen for its own sake and never as a means to something else.
notes: The concept of 'finality' (teleiotaton) introduces a ranking among ends that is crucial to identifying happiness as the chief good in the next passage. -- The examples of wealth and flutes as intermediate ends (chosen for the sake of something else) recall the earlier discussion of instrumental goods. -- This passage establishes the criterion of finality (teleiotes) that, together with self-sufficiency (autarkeia) in 1.16, forms Aristotle's formal definition of the highest good. -- The argument structure -- moving from multiple ends to a single supreme end -- mirrors the hierarchical structure of crafts established in 1.1.

refashioned: Now happiness, above all else, seems to be of this ultimate character. For we always choose happiness for its own sake and never for the sake of something else. Honor, pleasure, reason, and every virtue we do indeed choose for themselves -- we would choose each of them even if nothing further resulted from them -- but we also choose them for the sake of happiness, believing that through them we will be happy. Happiness, on the other hand, no one chooses for the sake of these things, nor for the sake of anything else at all.
elegant: Happiness fits this criterion perfectly: we always choose it for itself, never as a means to something else. Honor, pleasure, reason, and virtue we choose both for themselves and for the sake of happiness -- but no one chooses happiness for the sake of these or anything else.
summary: Happiness uniquely satisfies the criterion of finality. While we choose things like honor, pleasure, and virtue both for themselves and as means to happiness, we never choose happiness as a means to anything further.
distilled: Happiness alone is chosen purely for its own sake and never as a means to anything else.
notes: This passage applies the criterion of finality established in 1.14 to identify happiness (eudaimonia) as the chief good. -- The claim that we choose virtue 'for itself' but also 'for the sake of happiness' raises a question about whether virtue is intrinsically or instrumentally valuable; Aristotle's answer (both) is developed throughout the Ethics. -- The list of goods chosen for themselves (honor, pleasure, reason, virtue) corresponds to the candidate lives surveyed in 1.5-1.8. -- This passage is crucial for understanding that eudaimonia for Aristotle is not a subjective feeling but the most complete and self-sufficient activity.

refashioned:
The same conclusion seems to follow from the point of view of self-sufficiency, for the ultimate good is thought to be self-sufficient. By 'self-sufficient' we do not mean what suffices for a person living in isolation, but what suffices also for parents, children, spouse, and in general for friends and fellow citizens, since the human being is by nature a social and political creature. But some limit must be set to this, for if we extend the requirement to ancestors and descendants and friends of friends, we would be launched into an infinite series. Let us set this question aside for now.

We define self-sufficiency as that which, taken by itself, makes life choiceworthy and lacking in nothing. This, we hold, is what happiness is. Furthermore, we think happiness is the most choiceworthy of all things, but not as one good counted among others. If it were merely one good among many, it would obviously be made more choiceworthy by the addition of even the smallest further good, since what is added creates a surplus of goods, and the greater amount of good is always more choiceworthy. Happiness, then, is something ultimate and self-sufficient, and is the end of action.
elegant: The chief good must also be self-sufficient -- not for a solitary individual, but for someone living with family, friends, and fellow citizens, since humans are by nature political beings. Self-sufficiency means making life choiceworthy and complete in itself. Happiness meets this criterion, and it is not merely one good among others; if it were, adding any small good would make the combination more choiceworthy. Happiness is therefore ultimate, self-sufficient, and the end of action.
summary: Happiness is self-sufficient: it makes life fully choiceworthy on its own, needing nothing added. This self-sufficiency is understood not in terms of solitary living but in the context of family, friends, and civic life. Happiness is not one good among many but stands as the complete and final end of all action.
distilled: Happiness is the self-sufficient end that makes life complete, understood not in isolation but within the full context of human social life.
notes: The claim that humans are 'by nature political' (politikon zoon) is stated more fully in Politics I.2 (1253a2-3): 'Man is by nature a political animal.' -- The self-sufficiency (autarkeia) criterion complements the finality criterion from 1.14-1.15. Together they provide Aristotle's formal characterization of happiness. -- The argument that happiness is not 'one good among others' but rather an all-encompassing good anticipates the question of whether happiness is an 'inclusive' or 'dominant' end -- a major debate in Aristotelian scholarship (Ackrill vs. Kraut). -- The mention of the infinite regress concerning ancestors and descendants connects to the discussion in 1.10-1.11 about whether the fortunes of descendants affect one's happiness.

refashioned:
However, to say that happiness is the chief good is presumably a platitude, and a clearer account of what it is remains to be given. This might be achieved if we could first determine the function of a human being. For just as the good and the 'doing well' for a flute-player, a sculptor, or any craftsperson -- and in general for anything that has a function or characteristic activity -- is thought to reside in that function, so it would seem to be for a human being, if indeed a human being has a function.

Do the carpenter and the tanner have functions and activities, but the human being as such has none? Is a human being born without a function? Or, just as the eye, the hand, the foot, and each of the bodily parts evidently has a function, may we also attribute a function to the human being as a whole, over and above all these particular functions?

What, then, could this function be? Life -- in the sense of nutrition and growth -- seems to be shared even with plants, but we are seeking what is distinctive to human beings. Let us therefore set aside the life of nutrition and growth. Next would come the life of sense-perception, but this too appears to be shared with horses, cattle, and every animal. What remains, then, is an active life of the element that possesses reason. This rational element has two aspects: one that is rational in the sense of being obedient to reason, the other in the sense of possessing reason and actively thinking. And since 'the life of the rational element' can be understood in two ways, we must specify that we mean life in the sense of activity, for this seems to be the more proper sense of the term.
elegant: Saying happiness is the chief good is a platitude -- we need to determine what it consists in. This becomes possible if we identify the distinctive function of a human being. The good for anything with a function resides in performing that function well. Since nutrition belongs to plants and perception to animals, the distinctively human function must be an active life of the rational element -- not merely possessing reason but actively exercising it.
summary: To give real content to 'happiness,' Aristotle introduces the function argument: every being with a characteristic function achieves its good by performing that function well. The distinctively human function is rational activity -- not the life of nutrition shared with plants or perception shared with animals, but the active exercise of reason.
distilled: The distinctively human function is the active exercise of reason, and human good must therefore consist in performing this function well.
notes: The 'function argument' (ergon argument) is one of the most important and debated passages in the entire Ethics. -- The Greek word ergon means function, characteristic work, or activity; it carries teleological weight in Aristotle's biology and metaphysics. -- The elimination of nutrition and perception to arrive at reason as distinctive parallels De Anima II.2-3, where Aristotle distinguishes nutritive, perceptive, and rational soul. -- The distinction between possessing reason and exercising it (hexis vs. energeia) was prefigured in the critique of virtue-as-mere-possession in 1.7. -- The function argument has been criticized by many modern philosophers (e.g., J.L. Austin, Bernard Williams) who question whether humans have a function in the way that tools and organs do.

refashioned: Now if the function of a human being is an activity of the soul that expresses or involves a rational principle, and if the function of a given kind of person and of a good person of that kind are the same in type -- as the function of a lyre-player and a good lyre-player are the same in type, the difference being that excellence is added to the function (for the function of a lyre-player is to play the lyre, and of a good lyre-player to play it well) -- and if this holds without qualification in every case, with superiority in excellence being added to the function: then, if we identify the function of a human being as a certain kind of life, namely an activity or set of actions of the soul that express a rational principle, and the function of a good human being as performing these well and nobly; and if any action is well performed when it is performed in accordance with the appropriate excellence: then it follows that the human good is activity of the soul in accordance with virtue -- and if there are multiple virtues, in accordance with the best and most complete.
elegant: If the human function is rational activity of the soul, and a good person performs this function excellently -- just as a good lyre-player plays the lyre well -- then the human good is activity of the soul in accordance with virtue. If there are multiple virtues, it is activity in accordance with the best and most complete virtue.
summary: Since the human function is rational activity, and excellence means performing one's function well, the human good turns out to be activity of the soul in accordance with virtue -- or if there are several virtues, in accordance with the best and most complete one.
distilled: Human good is activity of the soul in accordance with the best and most complete virtue.
notes: This is Aristotle's formal definition of happiness (eudaimonia): 'activity of soul in accordance with virtue, and if there are more than one virtue, in accordance with the best and most complete.' -- The phrase 'best and most complete' (ariste kai teleiotate) raises the question of whether happiness requires all the virtues or only the highest one (contemplation). This debate runs through the entire Ethics and is addressed in Book X.7-8. -- 'Virtue' (arete) here means excellence of any kind, not merely moral goodness. -- The lyre-player analogy illustrates the principle that the good of any kind X is determined by what a good X does. -- This passage, together with 1.17, forms the core of the function argument, which Thomas Aquinas adopted and Christianized in the Summa Theologiae.

refashioned:
But we must add the qualification 'in a complete life.' For one swallow does not make a summer, nor does one day; and similarly, one day or a brief period does not make a person blessed and happy.

Let this serve as an outline of the good, for presumably one should first sketch it roughly and fill in the details later. It would seem that anyone is capable of carrying forward and articulating what has been well outlined, and that time is a good discoverer and collaborator in such work -- which is how advances in the arts and sciences have come about, since anyone can add what is lacking.

We must also keep in mind what was said earlier: we should not demand the same precision in every subject, but in each case only as much as the subject matter allows and as much as is appropriate to the inquiry. A carpenter and a geometer investigate the right angle in different ways: the carpenter only insofar as it is useful for the work, while the geometer inquires into what it is or what kind of thing it is, since the geometer is a seeker of truth. We must proceed in the same way in all other areas, so that secondary matters do not overshadow the main task.

Nor should we demand an explanation of the 'why' in every case. In some cases it is enough to establish the 'that' clearly, as with first principles. The fact is itself the starting point, the first principle. Some first principles we grasp by induction, some by perception, some by a kind of habituation, and others in yet other ways. We must try to investigate each set of principles in the way natural to it, and take care to define them well, since they have a great influence on everything that follows. For the starting point is thought to be more than half of the whole, and many of the questions we ask are answered once the starting point is established.
elegant: We must add: 'in a complete life.' One swallow does not make a summer, nor does one day of good action make a person happy. Let this serve as an outline to be filled in later. We must also remember not to demand equal precision in every subject: a carpenter and a geometer study the right angle differently. Nor must we always demand the 'why'; sometimes establishing the 'that' suffices, as with first principles, which we grasp through induction, perception, or habituation. First principles matter enormously, for the starting point is more than half the whole.
summary: Happiness requires a complete life, not just a moment of virtue. This outline of the good will be refined later. Different subjects require different levels of precision, and not everything needs a 'why' -- sometimes establishing the fact is enough. First principles, grasped through induction, perception, or habituation, are enormously important because they determine everything that follows.
distilled: Happiness requires not a single good day but a complete life of virtuous activity, and establishing sound first principles is more than half the work of any inquiry.
notes: The famous proverb 'one swallow does not make a summer' (mia chelidon ear ou poiei) remains in common use today. -- The qualification 'in a complete life' (en bioi teleioi) raises difficult questions about whether happiness can be attributed to someone before death, which Aristotle addresses in 1.10 (1.29-1.35). -- The distinction between the 'that' (to hoti) and the 'why' (to dioti) is fundamental to Aristotle's epistemology; see Posterior Analytics I.13. -- The claim that the beginning is 'more than half the whole' is attributed to various sources and became proverbial in Greek. -- The methodological remarks here echo those in 1.3 about appropriate precision.

refashioned:
We must, however, consider our definition not only in light of our conclusion and premises, but also in light of what is commonly said about happiness, since all the data harmonize with a true account, while a false one quickly clashes with the facts.

Now goods have been divided into three classes: external goods, goods of the body, and goods of the soul. Of these, we call goods of the soul the most properly and truly goods, and we classify actions and activities of the soul among goods of the soul. Therefore our account must be sound, at least according to this long-established view accepted by philosophers. It is also correct insofar as we identify the end with certain actions and activities, for this places the end among goods of the soul rather than external goods.

Another widely held belief that harmonizes with our account is that the happy person lives well and does well, for we have essentially defined happiness as a kind of living well and acting well.

Moreover, the characteristics that various thinkers look for in happiness all seem to belong to what we have defined it as. Some identify happiness with virtue, others with practical wisdom, others with a kind of philosophical wisdom, others with these accompanied by pleasure or at least not without pleasure, and still others include external prosperity. Some of these views have been held by many people over a long period, others by a few distinguished thinkers. It is unlikely that any of these groups is entirely wrong; rather, they are probably right in at least one respect, or even in most respects.
elegant: A true account of happiness should harmonize with common beliefs. The traditional division of goods into external, bodily, and psychical -- with psychical being highest -- confirms our definition, since we placed happiness among activities of the soul. The common belief that the happy person lives well and acts well also agrees. Furthermore, the various views that happiness involves virtue, practical wisdom, philosophical wisdom, pleasure, or external prosperity all capture some aspect of what we have defined happiness to be.
summary: Aristotle tests his definition of happiness against common opinions and philosophical tradition. The classification of goods into external, bodily, and psychical (with psychical highest) supports his account. So does the common equation of happiness with living and acting well. The various candidate definitions -- virtue, wisdom, pleasure, prosperity -- each capture some part of the truth.
distilled: A correct definition of happiness should harmonize with the best common opinions, and Aristotle's account -- activity of the soul in accordance with virtue -- does precisely this.
notes: The tripartite division of goods into external, bodily, and psychical was traditional in Greek philosophy; see also Plato's Laws V (743e). -- The dialectical method of testing a philosophical thesis against reputable opinions (endoxa) is described in Topics I.1 and is Aristotle's primary method in the Ethics. -- The phrase 'living well and doing well' (eu zen kai eu prattein) confirms the active nature of eudaimonia. -- The various identifications of happiness (with virtue, wisdom, pleasure, etc.) correspond to the views surveyed in 1.4-1.8, showing that Aristotle's definition synthesizes rather than simply rejects competing views.

refashioned:
Our account is in harmony with those who identify happiness with virtue or with some particular virtue, for virtuous activity belongs to virtue. But it makes no small difference whether we locate the chief good in the possession of virtue or in its exercise -- in the state of mind or in the activity. For a state of mind can exist without producing any good result, as in someone who is asleep or otherwise completely inactive. But this cannot be the case with activity: one who is engaged in activity will necessarily be acting, and acting well.

Just as in the Olympic Games it is not the most beautiful and strongest who are crowned, but those who actually compete (for it is among the competitors that the victors are found), so too in life: it is those who act rightly who win the noble and good things.
elegant: Our account agrees with those who identify happiness with virtue, but with a crucial distinction: happiness lies not in merely possessing virtue but in exercising it. A state can exist without producing any result -- a virtuous person can be asleep. But activity necessarily involves acting, and acting well. As at the Olympic Games, crowns go not to the strongest but to those who compete; so in life, noble prizes go to those who act.
summary: Happiness is not the mere possession of virtue but its active exercise. Just as Olympic crowns go to those who compete rather than to the strongest bystanders, the good things in life go to those who act virtuously, not to those who merely have the capacity for virtue.
distilled: Happiness lies not in possessing virtue but in actively exercising it, just as Olympic victory belongs to those who compete, not merely to the strongest.
notes: The Olympic Games analogy is one of Aristotle's most vivid illustrations. The ancient Olympic Games (held at Olympia every four years from 776 BCE) were the most prestigious athletic competition in Greece. -- The distinction between possession (hexis) and exercise (energeia/chresis) is fundamental to Aristotle's ethics and was foreshadowed in the critique of the merely virtuous but inactive person in 1.7. -- This passage reinforces that eudaimonia is an activity (energeia), not a state (hexis) -- a point with major implications for the entire ethical theory. -- Compare Metaphysics Theta 6-8 on the priority of actuality over potentiality.

refashioned:
The life of those who act virtuously is also pleasant in itself. For pleasure is a condition of the soul, and to each person that which they are said to love is pleasant: a horse is pleasant to the lover of horses, a spectacle to the lover of sights, and in the same way just actions are pleasant to the lover of justice, and virtuous actions generally to the lover of virtue.

For most people, their pleasures conflict with one another because these pleasures are not pleasant by nature. But for lovers of what is noble, the things that are naturally pleasant are what they find pleasant, and virtuous actions are of this kind. These actions are therefore pleasant both for such people and in their own nature. The life of the virtuous, then, has no need of pleasure as an added ornament but contains pleasure within itself.

Besides what we have said, a person who does not take delight in noble actions is not even good. No one would call a person just who did not enjoy acting justly, or generous who did not enjoy generous actions, and so on in every other case. If this is so, then virtuous actions must be pleasant in themselves. But they are also good and noble, and they possess each of these qualities to the highest degree, since the good person judges these matters correctly, and their judgment accords with what we have described.

Happiness, then, is the best, the noblest, and the most pleasant thing, and these qualities are not separated as in the inscription at Delos --
elegant: Virtuous activity is inherently pleasant. Each person finds pleasure in what they love, and the lover of virtue finds pleasure in virtuous acts. Unlike the conflicting pleasures of most people, the pleasures of the virtuous are naturally pleasant. Someone who takes no delight in noble action is not truly good -- no one is just who does not enjoy acting justly. Virtuous actions are therefore simultaneously good, noble, and pleasant to the highest degree. Happiness unites these qualities that the Delian inscription separates.
summary: Virtuous activity is inherently pleasant because people take pleasure in what they love, and lovers of virtue love virtuous action. Unlike ordinary pleasures that conflict with each other, the pleasure of virtue is natural and stable. Happiness thus unifies goodness, nobility, and pleasure -- qualities that popular opinion wrongly separates.
distilled: Virtuous activity is inherently pleasant because those who truly love the good find their deepest pleasure in noble action itself.
notes: The claim that virtuous action is pleasant 'in itself' (kath' hauto) is important: Aristotle is not saying that virtue is pleasant as a means to something else, but that the activity itself is enjoyable for the virtuous person. -- This anticipates the extended treatment of pleasure in Books VII.11-14 and X.1-5. -- The discussion of natural vs. non-natural pleasures connects to the account of 'pleasure proper to each activity' in Book X.5. -- The 'inscription at Delos' is quoted in the next passage (1.23). Delos was a sacred island in the Aegean, birthplace of Apollo and Artemis, and site of a major Greek temple and sanctuary.

refashioned:
'Most noble is that which is most just, and best is health;
but most pleasant is it to win what we love.'
elegant: 'Noblest is justice, best is health, most pleasant is winning what we love.'
summary: This inscription from the temple at Delos separates justice, health, and pleasure as three distinct goods -- a separation that Aristotle's account of happiness overcomes.
distilled: The Delian inscription separates nobility, health, and pleasure, but happiness unites them all.
notes: This inscription was reportedly carved at the temple of Artemis (or Apollo) on the island of Delos. -- Aristotle quotes it to show that popular thought separates qualities that his account of happiness unifies: the good, the noble, and the pleasant all belong to virtuous activity. -- The inscription is also quoted in the Eudemian Ethics I.1 (1214a5). -- Delos was one of the most important religious sanctuaries in ancient Greece and the center of the Delian League, an Athenian-led alliance.

refashioned:
For all these qualities -- goodness, nobility, and pleasure -- belong to the best activities, and these activities, or the single best among them, we identify with happiness.

Yet happiness evidently also requires external goods, as we said; for it is impossible, or at least not easy, to perform noble actions without the proper resources. In many actions we use friends, wealth, and political power as instruments. And there are certain things whose absence diminishes happiness -- things like good birth, fine children, and physical beauty. For a person who is extremely ugly, of low birth, solitary, or childless is not very likely to be happy, and perhaps even less so if they had utterly bad children or friends, or had lost good ones through death.

As we said, then, happiness seems to require this kind of external prosperity in addition to virtuous activity. This is why some people identify happiness with good fortune, while others identify it with virtue.
elegant: Goodness, nobility, and pleasure all belong to the best activities, which we identify with happiness. Yet happiness also requires external goods: noble action needs resources, friends, wealth, and political power as instruments. The absence of good birth, children, or beauty diminishes happiness. This is why some identify happiness with fortune and others with virtue.
summary: While happiness is primarily virtuous activity, it also requires external goods like friends, wealth, beauty, and good birth. Noble actions are difficult or impossible without proper resources. This dual requirement explains why some identify happiness with fortune and others with virtue.
distilled: Happiness is primarily virtuous activity but also requires external goods, since noble actions are difficult without resources, friends, and favorable circumstances.
notes: The role of external goods (ta ektos agatha) in happiness is one of the most debated topics in Aristotelian ethics. Aristotle never resolves the tension between his claim that happiness is self-sufficient and his admission that it requires external conditions. -- This passage qualifies the strong claims of the function argument by reintroducing the role of fortune (tuche). -- The examples of diminishing conditions (ugliness, low birth, childlessness) reflect the social values of 4th-century Athens. -- The tension between virtue and fortune as sources of happiness is explored further in 1.9-1.10 (1.25-1.35) and recurs in Book X. -- Stoic philosophers later rejected this aspect of Aristotle's view, arguing that virtue alone is sufficient for happiness.

refashioned:
This is also why the question is raised whether happiness is acquired through learning, through habituation, through some other kind of training, or whether it comes as a gift of divine providence or even by chance.

Now if there is any gift of the gods to human beings, it is reasonable that happiness should be god-given, and most surely god-given of all human things, since it is the best. But this question would perhaps be more appropriate to another inquiry.

Happiness seems, however -- even if it is not sent by the gods but comes through virtue and some process of learning or training -- to be among the most divine things. For that which is the prize and end of virtue seems to be the best thing in the world, and something divine and blessed.
elegant: Is happiness acquired through learning, habituation, training, divine gift, or chance? If the gods give anything to humans, happiness would be the most likely gift, being the best of human things. Yet even if happiness comes through virtue and learning rather than divine gift, it remains among the most divine things -- for the prize and end of virtue is the best and most blessed thing in the world.
summary: Whether happiness comes from divine gift, learning, or training, it is among the most divine of human goods. Even if it results from virtue and effort rather than from the gods, the prize and end of virtue is the noblest and most blessed thing.
distilled: Whether divinely given or earned through virtue and effort, happiness is the most divine and blessed of all human achievements.
notes: The question of whether happiness is god-given or humanly achievable engages a long Greek debate about the role of divine favor (theia moira) in human life; see Plato's Meno (99e-100a) on virtue as a divine gift. -- Aristotle's preference for happiness as something achievable through effort reflects his broader commitment to human agency and practical wisdom. -- The 'other inquiry' likely refers to theology or metaphysics. -- The characterization of happiness as 'divine and blessed' (theion kai makarion) uses language typically reserved for the gods, suggesting that happiness brings humans closest to the divine condition -- a theme developed in Book X.7-8.

refashioned: On this view, happiness would also be widely shared, for all who are not impaired in their capacity for virtue can win it through a certain kind of study and care. And if it is better to become happy through effort than by chance, it is reasonable to expect that this is how things actually are, since everything that depends on the workings of nature is by nature as good as it can be, and the same holds for everything that depends on craft or any rational cause -- especially when it depends on the best of all causes. To entrust the greatest and noblest thing to chance would be a deeply defective arrangement.
elegant: If happiness comes through effort, it would be widely accessible to all whose capacity for virtue is not impaired. And it is reasonable that happiness should come through effort rather than chance, since nature and craft always produce the best results they can -- and to leave the greatest human good to mere chance would be a deeply defective arrangement.
summary: If happiness is achievable through effort and learning rather than chance, it would be accessible to nearly everyone with the capacity for virtue. Nature and rational causes produce the best outcomes possible, so it would be unreasonable for the greatest human good to depend on luck.
distilled: Happiness should be achievable through effort rather than left to chance, because nature produces the best results it can, and the greatest good should not depend on luck.
notes: The argument that nature does nothing in vain and always aims at the best possible outcome is a fundamental principle of Aristotle's natural philosophy; see Physics II.8 and Parts of Animals I.1. -- The claim that happiness is 'widely shared' (koinon) reflects Aristotle's relatively democratic view of human potential, though he does restrict it to those not 'maimed' in their capacity for virtue. -- This passage implicitly argues against the view (associated with popular Greek religion) that happiness depends primarily on divine favor or luck. -- The emphasis on effort (epimeleia) and study (mathesis) connects to the practical orientation of the Ethics.

refashioned:
The answer to the question we have been asking is also clear from our definition of happiness, for we have said it is a virtuous activity of the soul of a certain kind. Of the remaining goods, some must necessarily be present as preconditions of happiness, while others are naturally cooperative and useful as instruments.

This also agrees with what we said at the outset: we stated that the end of political science is the best end, and political science devotes most of its effort to making citizens of a certain character -- namely, good people capable of noble actions.
elegant: Our definition confirms the answer: happiness is virtuous activity of the soul. Other goods serve either as preconditions or as useful instruments. This aligns with our opening claim that political science, which aims at the best end, devotes itself primarily to making citizens good and capable of noble action.
summary: The definition of happiness as virtuous soul-activity clarifies the role of other goods: some are preconditions, others are instruments. This confirms that political science, which aims at the highest good, rightly focuses on cultivating good character in citizens.
distilled: Happiness is virtuous activity of the soul, and other goods serve either as necessary preconditions or as helpful instruments for this activity.
notes: This passage ties together the function argument (1.17-1.18), the role of external goods (1.24), and the opening claim about political science (1.2). -- The distinction between preconditions and instruments of happiness corresponds roughly to necessary conditions and contributing causes. -- The connection to political science reinforces Aristotle's view that ethics and politics are inseparable; see Politics VII.1-3 for an extended discussion of the best life in relation to the best political community. -- The claim that political science 'makes citizens good' is central to Aristotle's understanding of legislation and education as the primary tools of political life.

refashioned:
It is natural, then, that we do not call an ox, a horse, or any other animal happy, since none of them is capable of sharing in the kind of activity we have described. For this same reason, a child is not happy either, for a child is not yet capable of such actions due to their age. When children are called happy, it is because of the hopes we have for them.

For happiness requires, as we have said, both complete virtue and a complete life. Many changes and all sorts of fortunes occur in the course of a life, and the most prosperous person may fall into great misfortunes in old age, as the stories tell of Priam in the Trojan cycle. No one calls happy a person who has experienced such fortunes and come to a wretched end.
elegant: We do not call animals happy because they cannot engage in rational virtuous activity. Nor are children happy, since they are not yet capable of such activity; when called happy, it is only in hope. Happiness requires both complete virtue and a complete life, for fortune can reverse even the most prosperous existence -- as happened to Priam, who fell from greatness to misery.
summary: Animals and children cannot be happy in the proper sense because they lack the capacity for mature rational activity. Happiness requires both complete virtue and a complete life, since even the most fortunate person can meet with catastrophe, as the story of Priam illustrates.
distilled: Happiness requires both the full development of virtue and a complete life, since fortune can reverse prosperity at any stage.
notes: Priam was the legendary king of Troy, who was wealthy and powerful but lost his sons (including Hector) and his city in the Trojan War, and was killed by Achilles' son Neoptolemus. His story was told in Homer's Iliad and in the poems of the Trojan Cycle. -- The exclusion of animals from happiness follows from the function argument: only beings with rational capacity can perform the distinctively human function. -- The exclusion of children is not about their moral worth but about their developmental stage; compare Politics VII.13 on education. -- The requirement of a 'complete life' (bios teleios) raises the 'Priam problem' that dominates the next several sections (1.29-1.35) and connects to Solon's famous admonition.

refashioned:
Must no one, then, be called happy while they are still alive? Must we, as Solon advised, wait to see the end? Even if we accept this principle, is the person actually happy when they are dead? Or is that not quite absurd, especially for us who define happiness as an activity?

But if we do not call the dead person happy, and if Solon's point is rather that one can only then safely call a person blessed as being finally beyond evils and misfortunes, this too raises problems. For both good and evil are thought to affect a dead person just as much as one who is alive but unaware of them -- consider honors and dishonors, and the good or bad fortunes of children and descendants.

This raises a further difficulty. Suppose a person has lived happily into old age and died a death worthy of their life: many misfortunes may still befall their descendants -- some may be good and enjoy the life they deserve, others may experience the opposite. And clearly, the degrees of relationship between these descendants and their ancestor may vary enormously. It would be strange if the dead person's happiness shifted with each of these changes, becoming happy at one time and wretched at another. But it would also be strange if the fortunes of descendants had no effect at all on their ancestors, even for some period of time.
elegant: Must we follow Solon and call no one happy while alive? But calling the dead happy seems absurd if happiness is an activity. Perhaps Solon means only that the dead are beyond misfortune -- yet both good and evil seem to affect the dead, through honors and the fortunes of descendants. It would be strange if a dead person's happiness fluctuated with every descendant's fortune, yet equally strange if descendants' lives had no effect at all.
summary: Solon's advice to call no one happy until death raises paradoxes. If happiness is an activity, the dead cannot be happy. But if the dead are beyond misfortune, we still face the puzzle that descendants' fortunes seem to affect them. Aristotle finds it equally problematic to say the dead person's happiness fluctuates with every change in their descendants' lives and to say it is entirely unaffected.
distilled: The question of whether happiness can only be judged after death raises deep puzzles about whether the dead can be affected by the fortunes of their descendants.
notes: Solon (c. 640-558 BCE) was an Athenian statesman, lawgiver, and one of the 'Seven Sages' of Greece. His famous exchange with Croesus, king of Lydia, is reported by Herodotus (Histories I.30-33): Solon refused to call Croesus happy, saying 'Count no man happy until he is dead.' -- The puzzle about whether the dead can be affected by the living connects to Greek beliefs about the afterlife and ancestral honor. -- This passage begins an extended discussion (1.29-1.35) of the relationship between happiness, fortune, and the completeness of a life. -- The problem anticipates modern discussions of posthumous harm and whether the dead can be wronged.

refashioned:
But we must return to our original difficulty, for considering it may help solve our present problem. If we must see the end and only then call a person happy -- not as being happy now, but as having been happy before -- surely this is a paradox. When the person is happy, we cannot truly say so, because we do not wish to call the living happy on account of the changes that may befall them, and because we have assumed happiness to be something permanent and not easily altered. Yet a single individual may experience many turns of fortune's wheel.

For clearly, if we were to follow the fluctuations of someone's fortune, we would often call the same person happy and then wretched again, making the happy person out to be a kind of chameleon, resting on no stable foundation.

Or is tracking fortunes entirely the wrong approach? Success or failure in life does not depend on fortune, but, as we said, human life requires some favorable fortune as an addition. What truly constitutes happiness or its opposite is virtuous activity or its absence.
elegant: If we can only call someone happy after death -- not as being happy but as having been so -- this is paradoxical: we cannot affirm happiness when it exists. Following fortune's fluctuations would make the happy person a chameleon on unstable ground. But perhaps tracking fortune is the wrong approach entirely. Happiness does not depend on fortune but on virtuous activity; external fortune is merely an addition to what truly constitutes happiness.
summary: Calling someone happy only after death creates a paradox: we cannot affirm their happiness while it exists. But tracking fortune's fluctuations would make happiness unstable and chameleon-like. The solution is that happiness depends fundamentally on virtuous activity, not on fortune, which is merely a supplement.
distilled: Happiness depends not on fortune's fluctuations but on the stability of virtuous activity, which is the true foundation of a good life.
notes: The 'chameleon' metaphor vividly captures the instability that would result from tying happiness to fortune. -- This passage resolves the Solon problem by distinguishing what constitutes happiness (virtuous activity) from what supplements it (fortune). -- The claim that virtuous activities 'constitute' happiness while external goods are 'additions' parallels the distinction between formal and material causes in Aristotle's metaphysics. -- This resolution anticipates the Stoic position that virtue is sufficient for happiness, though Aristotle does not go that far -- he maintains that external goods are genuinely needed, just not as the primary determinant.

refashioned:
The question we have just discussed confirms our definition. For no human activity has as much permanence as virtuous activities -- they are thought to be more enduring even than scientific knowledge. And among virtuous activities themselves, the most valuable are the most enduring, because happy people spend their lives most readily and most continuously engaged in these. This seems to be the reason we do not forget them.

The quality we seek, then, will belong to the happy person, and they will be happy throughout their life.
